From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Rupture in a planet's crust where material escapes

"Volcanic" redirects here. For other uses, see [Volcano (disambiguation)](/wiki/Volcano_(disambiguation) "Volcano (disambiguation)") and [Volcanic (disambiguation)](/wiki/Volcanic_(disambiguation) "Volcanic (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/Augustine_volcano_Jan_24_2006_-_Cyrus_Read.jpg/500px-Augustine_volcano_Jan_24_2006_-_Cyrus_Read.jpg)](/wiki/File:Augustine_volcano_Jan_24_2006_-_Cyrus_Read.jpg)

[Augustine Volcano](/wiki/Augustine_Volcano "Augustine Volcano") (Alaska) during its eruptive phase on January 24, 2006

A **volcano** is a vent or fissure in the [crust](/wiki/Crust_(geology) "Crust (geology)") of a [planetary-mass object](/wiki/Planetary-mass_object "Planetary-mass object"), such as [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth"), that allows hot [lava](/wiki/Lava "Lava"), [volcanic ash](/wiki/Volcanic_ash "Volcanic ash"), and [gases](/wiki/Volcanic_gas "Volcanic gas") to escape from a [magma chamber](/wiki/Magma_chamber "Magma chamber") below the surface.[[1]](#cite_note-gsa-1)

On Earth, volcanoes are most often found where [tectonic plates](/wiki/List_of_tectonic_plates "List of tectonic plates") are [diverging](/wiki/Divergent_boundary "Divergent boundary") or [converging](/wiki/Convergent_boundary "Convergent boundary"), and because most of Earth's plate boundaries are underwater, most volcanoes are found underwater. For example, a [mid-ocean ridge](/wiki/Mid-ocean_ridge "Mid-ocean ridge"), such as the [Mid-Atlantic Ridge](/wiki/Mid-Atlantic_Ridge "Mid-Atlantic Ridge"), has volcanoes caused by divergent tectonic plates whereas the Pacific [Ring of Fire](/wiki/Ring_of_Fire "Ring of Fire") has volcanoes caused by convergent tectonic plates. Volcanoes resulting from divergent tectonic activity are usually non-explosive whereas those resulting from convergent tectonic activity cause violent eruptions.[[2]](#cite_note-dkp-2)[[3]](#cite_note-mor-3) Volcanoes can also form where there is stretching and thinning of the crust's plates, such as in the [East African Rift](/wiki/East_African_Rift "East African Rift"), the [Wells Gray-Clearwater volcanic field](/wiki/Wells_Gray-Clearwater_volcanic_field "Wells Gray-Clearwater volcanic field"), and the [Rio Grande rift](/wiki/Rio_Grande_rift "Rio Grande rift") in North America. Volcanism away from plate boundaries most likely arises from upwelling [diapirs](/wiki/Diapir "Diapir") from the [core–mantle boundary](/wiki/Core%E2%80%93mantle_boundary "Core–mantle boundary") called [mantle plumes](/wiki/Mantle_plume "Mantle plume"), 3,000 kilometres (1,900 mi) deep within Earth. This results in [hotspot volcanism](/wiki/Hotspot_(geology) "Hotspot (geology)") or [intraplate volcanism](/wiki/Intraplate_volcanism "Intraplate volcanism"), in which the plume may cause thinning of the crust and result in a [volcanic island](/wiki/Volcanic_island "Volcanic island") chain due to the continuous movement of the tectonic plate, of which the [Hawaiian hotspot](/wiki/Hawaiian_hotspot "Hawaiian hotspot") is an example.[[4]](#cite_note-bgs-4) Volcanoes are usually not created at [transform tectonic boundaries](/wiki/Transform_fault "Transform fault") where two tectonic plates slide past one another.

Volcanoes, based on their frequency of eruption or volcanism, are referred to as either [active](/wiki/Active_volcano "Active volcano"), dormant, or extinct.[[5]](#cite_note-www.usgs.gov2-5) Active volcanoes have a history of volcanism and are likely to erupt again, while extinct ones are not capable of eruption at all as they have no magma source. "Dormant" volcanoes have not erupted in a long time – generally accepted as since the start of the [Holocene](/wiki/Holocene "Holocene"), about 12,000 years ago – but may erupt again. However, dormant volcanoes are technically considered to be seismically "active".[[5]](#cite_note-www.usgs.gov2-5) These categories aren't entirely uniform; they may overlap for certain examples.[[2]](#cite_note-dkp-2)[[6]](#cite_note-ade-6)[[7]](#cite_note-ng-7)

Large eruptions can affect atmospheric temperature as ash and droplets of [sulfuric acid](/wiki/Sulfuric_acid "Sulfuric acid") obscure the Sun and cool Earth's [troposphere](/wiki/Troposphere "Troposphere"). Historically, large volcanic eruptions have been followed by [volcanic winters](/wiki/Volcanic_winter "Volcanic winter") which have caused catastrophic famines.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Other planets besides Earth have volcanoes. For example, volcanoes are very numerous on Venus.[[9]](#cite_note-9) Mars has significant volcanoes.[[10]](#cite_note-10) In 2009, a paper was published suggesting a new definition for the word 'volcano' that includes processes such as [cryovolcanism](/wiki/Cryovolcanism "Cryovolcanism"). It suggested that a volcano be defined as 'an opening on a planet or moon's surface from which [magma](/wiki/Magma "Magma"), as defined for that body, and/or magmatic gas is erupted.'[[11]](#cite_note-11)

This article mainly covers volcanoes on Earth. See [§ Volcanoes on other celestial bodies](#Volcanoes_on_other_celestial_bodies) and [cryovolcano](/wiki/Cryovolcano "Cryovolcano") for more information.

## Etymology and terminology

The word *volcano* ([UK](/wiki/British_English "British English"): [/vɒlˈkeɪnəʊ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); [US](/wiki/American_English "American English"): [/vɑːlˈkeɪnoʊ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English")) originates from the early [17th century](/wiki/17th_century "17th century"), derived from the Italian name *[Vulcano](/wiki/Vulcano "Vulcano")*, a [volcanic island](/wiki/Volcanic_island "Volcanic island") in the [Aeolian Islands](/wiki/Aeolian_Islands "Aeolian Islands") of Italy, which in turn comes from the [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") name *[Volcānus](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Volcanus#Latin "wikt:Volcanus")* or *[Vulcānus](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Vulcanus#Latin "wikt:Vulcanus")*, referring to [Vulcan](/wiki/Vulcan_(mythology) "Vulcan (mythology)"), the god of fire in [Roman mythology](/wiki/Roman_mythology "Roman mythology").[[12]](#cite_note-oed-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

The set of processes and phenomena involved in volcanic activity is called [volcanism](/wiki/Volcanism "Volcanism") [early [19th century](/wiki/19th_century "19th century"): from *volcano* + *-ism*]. The study of volcanism and volcanoes is called [volcanology](/wiki/Volcanology "Volcanology") [mid-19th century: from *volcano* + *-logy*], sometimes spelled *vulcanology*.[[12]](#cite_note-oed-12)

## Plate tectonics

Main article: [Plate tectonics](/wiki/Plate_tectonics "Plate tectonics")

According to the theory of plate tectonics, Earth's [lithosphere](/wiki/Lithosphere "Lithosphere"), its rigid outer shell, is broken into sixteen larger and several smaller plates. These move continuously at a slow pace, due to [convection](/wiki/Convection "Convection") in the underlying ductile [mantle](/wiki/Earth%27s_mantle "Earth's mantle"), and most volcanic activity on Earth takes place along plate boundaries, where plates are converging (and lithosphere is being destroyed) or are diverging (and new lithosphere is being created).[[14]](#cite_note-14)

During the development of geological theory, certain concepts that allowed the grouping of volcanoes in time, place, structure and composition have developed that ultimately have had to be explained in the theory of plate tectonics. For example, some volcanoes are *[polygenetic](/wiki/Polygenetic_volcanic_field "Polygenetic volcanic field")* with more than one period of activity during their history; other volcanoes that become extinct after erupting exactly once are *[monogenetic](/wiki/Monogenetic_volcanic_field "Monogenetic volcanic field")* (meaning "one life") and such volcanoes are often grouped together in a geographical region.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

### Divergent plate boundaries

Main article: [Divergent boundary](/wiki/Divergent_boundary "Divergent boundary")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/Spreading_ridges_volcanoes_map-en.svg/500px-Spreading_ridges_volcanoes_map-en.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Spreading_ridges_volcanoes_map-en.svg)

Map showing the divergent plate boundaries (oceanic spreading ridges) and recent sub-aerial volcanoes (mostly at convergent boundaries)

At the [mid-ocean ridges](/wiki/Mid-ocean_ridge "Mid-ocean ridge"), two [tectonic plates](/wiki/List_of_tectonic_plates "List of tectonic plates") diverge from one another as hot mantle rock creeps upwards beneath the thinned [oceanic crust](/wiki/Oceanic_crust "Oceanic crust"). The decrease of pressure in the rising mantle rock leads to [adiabatic](/wiki/Adiabatic "Adiabatic") expansion and the [partial melting](/wiki/Partial_melting "Partial melting") of the rock, causing volcanism and creating new oceanic crust. Most [divergent plate boundaries](/wiki/Divergent_plate_boundaries "Divergent plate boundaries") are at the bottom of the oceans, and so most volcanic activity on Earth is submarine, forming new [seafloor](/wiki/Seafloor "Seafloor"). [Black smokers](/wiki/Black_smoker "Black smoker") (also known as deep sea vents) are evidence of this kind of volcanic activity. Where the mid-oceanic ridge is above sea level, volcanic islands are formed, such as [Iceland](/wiki/Iceland "Iceland").[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchmincke200317–18,_276-16)[[3]](#cite_note-mor-3)

### Convergent plate boundaries

Main article: [Convergent boundary](/wiki/Convergent_boundary "Convergent boundary")

[Subduction](/wiki/Subduction "Subduction") zones are places where two plates, usually an oceanic plate and a continental plate, collide. The oceanic plate subducts (dives beneath the continental plate), forming a deep ocean trench just offshore. In a process called [flux melting](/wiki/Flux_melting "Flux melting"), water released from the subducting plate lowers the melting temperature of the overlying mantle wedge, thus creating [magma](/wiki/Magma "Magma"). This magma tends to be extremely [viscous](/wiki/Viscous "Viscous") because of its high [silica](/wiki/Silica "Silica") content, so it often does not reach the surface but [cools and solidifies at depth](/wiki/Igneous_intrusion "Igneous intrusion"). When it does reach the surface, however, a volcano is formed. Thus subduction zones are bordered by chains of volcanoes called [volcanic arcs](/wiki/Volcanic_arc "Volcanic arc"). Typical examples are the volcanoes in the Pacific [Ring of Fire](/wiki/Ring_of_Fire "Ring of Fire"), such as the [Cascade Volcanoes](/wiki/Cascade_Volcanoes "Cascade Volcanoes") or the [Japanese Archipelago](/wiki/Japanese_Archipelago "Japanese Archipelago"), or the eastern islands of [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia").[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchmincke200318,_113–126-17)[[2]](#cite_note-dkp-2)

### Hotspots

Main article: [Hotspot (geology)](/wiki/Hotspot_(geology) "Hotspot (geology)")

[Hotspots](/wiki/Hotspot_(geology) "Hotspot (geology)") are volcanic areas thought to be formed by [mantle plumes](/wiki/Mantle_plume "Mantle plume"), which are hypothesized to be columns of hot material rising from the core-mantle boundary. As with mid-ocean ridges, the rising mantle rock experiences decompression melting which generates large volumes of magma. Because tectonic plates move across mantle plumes, each volcano becomes inactive as it drifts off the plume, and new volcanoes are created where the plate advances over the plume. The [Hawaiian Islands](/wiki/Hawaiian_Islands "Hawaiian Islands") are thought to have been formed in such a manner, as has the [Snake River Plain](/wiki/Snake_River_Plain "Snake River Plain"), with the [Yellowstone Caldera](/wiki/Yellowstone_Caldera "Yellowstone Caldera") being part of the North American plate currently above the [Yellowstone hotspot](/wiki/Yellowstone_hotspot "Yellowstone hotspot").[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchmincke200318,_106–107-18)[[4]](#cite_note-bgs-4) However, the mantle plume hypothesis has been questioned.[[19]](#cite_note-Foulger-19)

### Continental rifting

Main article: [Rift](/wiki/Rift "Rift")

Sustained upwelling of hot mantle rock can develop under the interior of a continent and lead to rifting. Early stages of rifting are characterized by [flood basalts](/wiki/Flood_basalt "Flood basalt") and may progress to the point where a tectonic plate is completely split.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchmincke2003108–110-21) A divergent plate boundary then develops between the two halves of the split plate. However, rifting often fails to completely split the continental lithosphere (such as in an [aulacogen](/wiki/Aulacogen "Aulacogen")), and failed rifts are characterized by volcanoes that erupt unusual [alkali lava](/wiki/Alkaline_magma_series "Alkaline magma series") or [carbonatites](/wiki/Carbonatite "Carbonatite"). Examples include the volcanoes of the [East African Rift](/wiki/East_African_Rift "East African Rift").[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPhilpottsAgue2009390–394,_396–397-22)

## Volcanic features

Further information: [Types of volcanoes](/wiki/Types_of_volcanoes "Types of volcanoes")

Video of lava agitating and bubbling in the volcanic eruption of Litli-Hrútur ([Fagradalsfjall](/wiki/Fagradalsfjall "Fagradalsfjall")), Iceland, 2023

A volcano needs a reservoir of molten magma (e.g. a [magma chamber](/wiki/Magma_chamber "Magma chamber")), a conduit to allow magma to rise through the crust, and a vent to allow the magma to escape above the surface as lava. The erupted volcanic material (lava and tephra) that is deposited around the vent is known as a **volcanic edifice**, typically a volcanic cone or mountain.[[2]](#cite_note-dkp-2)[[23]](#cite_note-NPS-Anat-23)

The most common perception of a volcano is of a [conical](/wiki/Conical "Conical") mountain, spewing [lava](/wiki/Lava "Lava") and poisonous [gases](/wiki/Volcanic_gas "Volcanic gas") from a [crater](/wiki/Volcanic_crater "Volcanic crater") at its summit; however, this describes just one of the many types of volcano. The features of volcanoes are varied. The structure and behaviour of volcanoes depend on several factors. Some volcanoes have rugged peaks formed by [lava domes](/wiki/Lava_dome "Lava dome") rather than a summit crater while others have [landscape](/wiki/Landscape "Landscape") features such as massive [plateaus](/wiki/Plateau "Plateau"). Vents that issue volcanic material (including lava and [ash](/wiki/Volcanic_ash "Volcanic ash")) and gases (mainly steam and magmatic gases) can develop anywhere on the [landform](/wiki/Landform "Landform") and may give rise to smaller cones such as [Puʻu ʻŌʻō](/wiki/Pu%CA%BBu_%CA%BB%C5%8C%CA%BB%C5%8D "Puʻu ʻŌʻō") on a flank of [Kīlauea](/wiki/K%C4%ABlauea "Kīlauea") in Hawaii. [Volcanic craters](/wiki/Volcanic_crater "Volcanic crater") are not always at the top of a mountain or hill and may be filled with lakes such as with [Lake Taupō](/wiki/Lake_Taup%C5%8D "Lake Taupō") in New Zealand. Some volcanoes can be low-relief landform features, with the potential to be hard to recognize as such and be obscured by geological processes.[[2]](#cite_note-dkp-2)[[24]](#cite_note-usgs-24)[[25]](#cite_note-taupo-25)

Other types of volcano include [mud volcanoes](/wiki/Mud_volcano "Mud volcano"), which are structures often not associated with known magmatic activity; and [cryovolcanoes](/wiki/Cryovolcano "Cryovolcano") (or ice volcanoes), particularly on some moons of [Jupiter](/wiki/Jupiter "Jupiter"), [Saturn](/wiki/Saturn "Saturn"), and [Neptune](/wiki/Neptune "Neptune"). Active mud volcanoes tend to involve temperatures much lower than those of [igneous](/wiki/Igneous "Igneous") volcanoes except when the mud volcano is actually a vent of an igneous volcano.

### Fissure vents

Main article: [Fissure vent](/wiki/Fissure_vent "Fissure vent")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Lakagigar_Iceland_2004-07-01.jpg/250px-Lakagigar_Iceland_2004-07-01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lakagigar_Iceland_2004-07-01.jpg)

[Lakagigar](/wiki/Laki "Laki") fissure vent in [Iceland](/wiki/Iceland "Iceland"), the source of the [major world climate alteration of 1783–84](/wiki/Laki#Consequences_in_Iceland "Laki"), has a chain of volcanic cones along its length.

[Volcanic fissure vents](/wiki/Fissure_vent "Fissure vent") are generally found at [diverging plate boundaries](/wiki/Divergent_boundary "Divergent boundary"), they are flat, linear fractures through which [basaltic lava](/wiki/Basaltic_lava "Basaltic lava") emerges. These kinds of volcanoes are non-explosive and the basaltic lava tends to have a low viscosity and solidifies slowly leading to a gentle sloping basaltic [lava plateau](/wiki/Volcanic_plateau "Volcanic plateau"). They often relate or constitute shield volcanoes[[2]](#cite_note-dkp-2)[[26]](#cite_note-nps-26)

### Shield volcanoes

Main article: [Shield volcano](/wiki/Shield_volcano "Shield volcano")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Skjaldbreidur_Herbst_2004.jpg/250px-Skjaldbreidur_Herbst_2004.jpg)](/wiki/File:Skjaldbreidur_Herbst_2004.jpg)

[Skjaldbreiður](/wiki/Skjaldbrei%C3%B0ur "Skjaldbreiður"), a shield volcano whose name means "broad shield"

Shield volcanoes, so named for their broad, shield-like profiles, are formed by the eruption of low-viscosity basaltic or andesitic lava that can flow a great distance from a vent. They generally do not explode catastrophically but are characterized by relatively gentle [effusive eruptions](/wiki/Effusive_eruption "Effusive eruption").[[2]](#cite_note-dkp-2) Since low-viscosity magma is typically low in silica, shield volcanoes are more common in oceanic than continental settings. The Hawaiian volcanic chain is a series of shield cones, and they are common in [Iceland](/wiki/Iceland "Iceland"), as well.[[26]](#cite_note-nps-26) [Olympus Mons](/wiki/Olympus_Mons "Olympus Mons"), an extinct martian shield volcano is the largest known volcano in the [Solar System](/wiki/Solar_System "Solar System").[[27]](#cite_note-msv-27)

### Lava domes

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/East_Dome_Mount_St._Helens.jpg/250px-East_Dome_Mount_St._Helens.jpg)](/wiki/File:East_Dome_Mount_St._Helens.jpg)

East dome, a lava dome located on the lower east flank of St. Helens, part of the Sugar Bowl Eruptive Period (1800 YA).

Main article: [Lava dome](/wiki/Lava_dome "Lava dome")

[Lava domes](/wiki/Lava_dome "Lava dome"), also called dome volcanoes, have steep convex sides built by slow eruptions of highly viscous lava, for example, [rhyolite](/wiki/Rhyolite "Rhyolite").[[2]](#cite_note-dkp-2) They are sometimes formed within the crater of a previous volcanic eruption, as in the case of [Mount St. Helens](/wiki/Mount_St._Helens "Mount St. Helens"), but can also form independently, as in the case of [Lassen Peak](/wiki/Lassen_Peak "Lassen Peak"). Like stratovolcanoes, they can produce violent, explosive eruptions, but the lava generally does not flow far from the originating vent.

### Cryptodomes

Cryptodomes are formed when viscous lava is forced upward causing the surface to bulge. The [1980 eruption of Mount St. Helens](/wiki/1980_eruption_of_Mount_St._Helens "1980 eruption of Mount St. Helens") was an example; lava beneath the surface of the mountain created an upward bulge, which later collapsed down the north side of the mountain.

### Cinder cones

Main articles: [Volcanic cone](/wiki/Volcanic_cone "Volcanic cone") and [Cinder cone](/wiki/Cinder_cone "Cinder cone")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Izalco_Volcano.jpg/250px-Izalco_Volcano.jpg)](/wiki/File:Izalco_Volcano.jpg)

[Izalco volcano](/wiki/Izalco_(volcano) "Izalco (volcano)"), the youngest volcano in El Salvador. Izalco erupted almost continuously from 1770 (when it formed) to 1958, earning it the nickname of "Lighthouse of the Pacific".

Cinder cones result from eruptions of mostly small pieces of [scoria](/wiki/Scoria "Scoria") and [pyroclastics](/wiki/Pyroclastic_rock "Pyroclastic rock") (both resemble cinders, hence the name of this volcano type) that build up around the vent. These can be relatively short-lived eruptions that produce a cone-shaped hill perhaps 30 to 400 metres (100 to 1,300 ft) high. Most cinder cones erupt only once and some may be found in [monogenetic volcanic fields](/wiki/Monogenetic_volcanic_field "Monogenetic volcanic field") that may include other features that form when magma comes into contact with water such as [maar](/wiki/Maar "Maar") explosion craters and [tuff rings](/wiki/Tuff_ring "Tuff ring").[[28]](#cite_note-Thomas2000-28) Cinder cones may form as [flank vents](/wiki/Parasitic_cone "Parasitic cone") on larger volcanoes, or occur on their own. [Parícutin](/wiki/Par%C3%ADcutin "Parícutin") in Mexico and [Sunset Crater](/wiki/Sunset_Crater "Sunset Crater") in [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona "Arizona") are examples of cinder cones. In [New Mexico](/wiki/New_Mexico "New Mexico"), [Caja del Rio](/wiki/Caja_del_Rio "Caja del Rio") is a [volcanic field](/wiki/Volcanic_field "Volcanic field") of over 60 cinder cones.

Based on satellite images, it has been suggested that cinder cones might occur on other terrestrial bodies in the Solar system too; on the surface of Mars and the Moon.[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-Ulysses-31)[[32]](#cite_note-mesic-32)

### Stratovolcanoes (composite volcanoes)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Volcano_scheme.svg/500px-Volcano_scheme.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Volcano_scheme.svg)

Cross-section through a [stratovolcano](/wiki/Stratovolcano "Stratovolcano") (vertical scale is exaggerated):

1. Large magma chamber
2. Bedrock
3. Conduit (pipe)
4. Base
5. Sill
6. Dike
7. Layers of ash emitted by the volcano
8. Flank
9. Layers of lava emitted by the volcano
10. Throat
11. Parasitic cone
12. Lava flows
13. Vents
14. Crater
15. Ash clouds

Main article: [Stratovolcano](/wiki/Stratovolcano "Stratovolcano")

Stratovolcanoes are tall conical mountains composed of lava flows and [tephra](/wiki/Tephra "Tephra") in alternate layers, the [strata](/wiki/Strata "Strata") that gives rise to the name. They are also known as *composite volcanoes* because they are created from multiple structures during different kinds of eruptions; the main conduit bringing magma to the surface branches into multiple secondary conduits and occasional [laccoliths](/wiki/Laccolith "Laccolith") or [sills](/wiki/Sill_(geology) "Sill (geology)"), the branching conduits may form [parasitic cones](/wiki/Parasitic_cone "Parasitic cone") on the flanks of the main cone.[[2]](#cite_note-dkp-2) Classic examples include [Mount Fuji](/wiki/Mount_Fuji "Mount Fuji") in Japan, [Mayon Volcano](/wiki/Mayon_Volcano "Mayon Volcano") in the Philippines, and [Mount Vesuvius](/wiki/Mount_Vesuvius "Mount Vesuvius") and [Stromboli](/wiki/Stromboli "Stromboli") in Italy.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Mount_Vesuvius_Araucaria.jpg/250px-Mount_Vesuvius_Araucaria.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mount_Vesuvius_Araucaria.jpg)

Mt. Vesuvius, a stratovolcano, [Gulf of Naples](/wiki/Gulf_of_Naples "Gulf of Naples").

[Ash](/wiki/Volcanic_ash "Volcanic ash") produced by the [explosive eruption](/wiki/Explosive_eruption "Explosive eruption") of stratovolcanoes has [historically](/wiki/Recorded_history "Recorded history") posed the greatest volcanic hazard to civilizations. The lavas of stratovolcanoes are higher in silica, and therefore much more viscous, than lavas from shield volcanoes. High-silica lavas also tend to contain more dissolved gas. The combination is deadly, promoting [explosive eruptions](/wiki/Explosive_eruption "Explosive eruption") that produce great quantities of ash, as well as [pyroclastic surges](/wiki/Pyroclastic_surge "Pyroclastic surge") like the one that destroyed the city of Saint-Pierre in Martinique in 1902. They are also steeper than shield volcanoes, with slopes of 30–35° compared to slopes of generally 5–10°, and their loose [tephra](/wiki/Tephra "Tephra") are material for dangerous [lahars](/wiki/Lahar "Lahar").[[33]](#cite_note-33) Large pieces of tephra are called [volcanic bombs](/wiki/Volcanic_bomb "Volcanic bomb"). Big bombs can measure more than 1.2 metres (4 ft) across and weigh several tons.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

### Supervolcanoes

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Lake_Taupo_%2831071970884%29.jpg/250px-Lake_Taupo_%2831071970884%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lake_Taupo_(31071970884).jpg)

Lake Taupō, a volcanogenic lake in the caldera of [Taupō supervolcano](/wiki/Taup%C5%8D_Volcano "Taupō Volcano"), New Zealand.

Main article: [Supervolcano](/wiki/Supervolcano "Supervolcano")

See also: [List of largest volcanic eruptions](/wiki/List_of_largest_volcanic_eruptions "List of largest volcanic eruptions")

A supervolcano is defined as a volcano that has experienced one or more eruptions that produced over 1,000 cubic kilometres (240 cu mi) of volcanic deposits in a single explosive event.[[35]](#cite_note-35) Such eruptions occur when a very large magma chamber full of gas-rich, silicic magma is emptied in a catastrophic [caldera](/wiki/Caldera "Caldera")-forming eruption. Ash flow [tuffs](/wiki/Tuff "Tuff") emplaced by such eruptions are the only volcanic product with volumes rivalling those of [flood basalts](/wiki/Flood_basalt "Flood basalt").[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPhilpottsAgue200977-36)

Supervolcano eruptions, while the most dangerous type, are very rare; [four are known from the last million years](/wiki/Supervolcano#Known_super_eruptions "Supervolcano"), and about 60 historical VEI 8 eruptions have been identified in the geologic record over millions of years. A supervolcano can produce devastation on a continental scale, and severely cool global temperatures for many years after the eruption due to the huge volumes of [sulfur](/wiki/Sulfur "Sulfur") and ash released into the atmosphere.

Because of the enormous area they cover, and subsequent concealment under vegetation and glacial deposits, supervolcanoes can be difficult to identify in the geologic record without careful [geological mapping](/wiki/Geological_map "Geological map").[[37]](#cite_note-37) Known examples include [Yellowstone Caldera](/wiki/Yellowstone_Caldera "Yellowstone Caldera") in [Yellowstone National Park](/wiki/Yellowstone_National_Park "Yellowstone National Park") and [Valles Caldera](/wiki/Valles_Caldera "Valles Caldera") in [New Mexico](/wiki/New_Mexico "New Mexico") (both western United States); [Lake Taupō](/wiki/Lake_Taup%C5%8D "Lake Taupō") in New Zealand; [Lake Toba](/wiki/Lake_Toba "Lake Toba") in [Sumatra](/wiki/Sumatra "Sumatra"), Indonesia; and [Ngorongoro Crater](/wiki/Ngorongoro_Crater "Ngorongoro Crater") in Tanzania.

#### Caldera volcanoes

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Crater_Lake_winter_pano2.jpg/250px-Crater_Lake_winter_pano2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Crater_Lake_winter_pano2.jpg)

[Crater Lake](/wiki/Crater_Lake "Crater Lake"), a volcanic lake in [Oregon](/wiki/Oregon "Oregon")

Volcanoes that, though large, are not large enough to be called supervolcanoes, may also form calderas (collapsed crater) in the same way. There may be active or dormant cones inside of the caldera or even a lake, such lakes are called [Volcanogenic lakes](/wiki/Volcanogenic_lake "Volcanogenic lake"), or simply, volcanic lakes.[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[2]](#cite_note-dkp-2)

### Submarine volcanoes

Main article: [Submarine volcano](/wiki/Submarine_volcano "Submarine volcano")

See also: [Subaqueous volcano](/wiki/Subaqueous_volcano "Subaqueous volcano")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/Tonga_Volcano_Eruption_2022-01-15_0320Z_to_0610Z_Himawari-8_visible.gif/250px-Tonga_Volcano_Eruption_2022-01-15_0320Z_to_0610Z_Himawari-8_visible.gif)](/wiki/File:Tonga_Volcano_Eruption_2022-01-15_0320Z_to_0610Z_Himawari-8_visible.gif)

Satellite images of the January 15, 2022, eruption of [Hunga Tonga-Hunga Haʻapai](/wiki/Hunga_Tonga-Hunga_Ha%CA%BBapai "Hunga Tonga-Hunga Haʻapai")

Submarine volcanoes are common features of the ocean floor. Volcanic activity during the [Holocene](/wiki/Holocene "Holocene") Epoch has been documented at only 119 submarine volcanoes, but there may be more than one million geologically young submarine volcanoes on the ocean floor.[[39]](#cite_note-GVPDatabase2020-39)[[40]](#cite_note-GVP-FAQ-40) In shallow water, active volcanoes disclose their presence by blasting steam and rocky debris high above the ocean's surface. In the deep ocean basins, the tremendous weight of the water prevents the explosive release of steam and gases; however, submarine eruptions can be detected by [hydrophones](/wiki/Hydrophone "Hydrophone") and by the discoloration of water because of [volcanic gases](/wiki/Volcanic_gas "Volcanic gas"). [Pillow lava](/wiki/Pillow_lava "Pillow lava") is a common eruptive product of submarine volcanoes and is characterized by thick sequences of discontinuous pillow-shaped masses which form underwater. Even large submarine eruptions may not disturb the ocean surface, due to the rapid cooling effect and increased buoyancy in water (as compared to air), which often causes volcanic vents to form steep pillars on the ocean floor. [Hydrothermal vents](/wiki/Hydrothermal_vent "Hydrothermal vent") are common near these volcanoes, and [some support peculiar ecosystems](/wiki/Black_smoker "Black smoker") based on [chemotrophs](/wiki/Chemotroph "Chemotroph") feeding on dissolved minerals. Over time, the formations created by submarine volcanoes may become so large that they break the ocean surface as new islands or floating [pumice rafts](/wiki/Pumice_raft "Pumice raft").

In May and June 2018, a multitude of [seismic](/wiki/Seismic "Seismic") signals were detected by [earthquake](/wiki/Earthquake "Earthquake") monitoring agencies all over the world. They took the form of unusual humming sounds, and some of the signals detected in November of that year had a duration of up to 20 minutes. An [oceanographic](/wiki/Oceanographic "Oceanographic") research campaign in May 2019 showed that the previously mysterious humming noises were caused by the formation of a submarine volcano off the coast of [Mayotte](/wiki/Mayotte "Mayotte").[[41]](#cite_note-41)

### Subglacial volcanoes

Main article: [Subglacial volcano](/wiki/Subglacial_volcano "Subglacial volcano")

Subglacial volcanoes develop underneath [ice caps](/wiki/Ice_cap "Ice cap"). They are made up of lava plateaus capping extensive pillow lavas and [palagonite](/wiki/Palagonite "Palagonite"). These volcanoes are also called table mountains, [tuyas](/wiki/Tuya "Tuya"),[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPhilpottsAgue200966-42) or (in Iceland) mobergs.[[43]](#cite_note-43) Very good examples of this type of volcano can be seen in Iceland and in [British Columbia](/wiki/British_Columbia "British Columbia"). The origin of the term comes from [Tuya Butte](/wiki/Tuya_Butte "Tuya Butte"), which is one of the several tuyas in the area of the [Tuya River](/wiki/Tuya_River "Tuya River") and [Tuya Range](/wiki/Tuya_Range "Tuya Range") in northern British Columbia. Tuya Butte was the first such [landform](/wiki/Landform "Landform") analysed and so its name has entered the geological literature for this kind of volcanic formation.[[44]](#cite_note-44) The [Tuya Mountains Provincial Park](/wiki/Tuya_Mountains_Provincial_Park "Tuya Mountains Provincial Park") was recently established to protect this unusual landscape, which lies north of [Tuya Lake](/wiki/Tuya_Lake "Tuya Lake") and south of the [Jennings River](/wiki/Jennings_River "Jennings River") near the boundary with the [Yukon Territory](/wiki/Yukon_Territory "Yukon Territory").

### Hydrothermal features

Hydrothermal features, for example [geysers](/wiki/Geyser "Geyser"), [fumaroles](/wiki/Fumarole "Fumarole"), [mud pools](/wiki/Mudpot "Mudpot"), [mud volcanoes](/wiki/Mud_volcano "Mud volcano"), [hot springs](/wiki/Hot_spring "Hot spring") and acidic hot springs involve water as well as geothermal or magmatic activity. Such features are common around volcanoes and are often indicative of volcanism.[[2]](#cite_note-dkp-2)[[45]](#cite_note-sd-45)

#### Mud volcanoes

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Gobustan_mud_volcano.jpg/250px-Gobustan_mud_volcano.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gobustan_mud_volcano.jpg)

Mud volcano at [Gobustan](/wiki/Gobustan_State_Historical_and_Cultural_Reserve "Gobustan State Historical and Cultural Reserve")

Main article: [Mud volcano](/wiki/Mud_volcano "Mud volcano")

[Mud volcanoes](/wiki/Mud_volcano "Mud volcano") or mud domes are conical structures created by eruption of liquids and gases, particularly mud (slurries), water and gases, although several activities may contribute. The largest mud volcanoes are 10 kilometres (6.2 mi) in diameter and reach 700 metres (2,300 ft) high.[[46]](#cite_note-46)[[47]](#cite_note-47) Mud volcanoes can be seen off the shore of [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia"), on the island of [Baratang](/wiki/Baratang_Island "Baratang Island"), in [Balochistan](/wiki/Balochistan,_Pakistan "Balochistan, Pakistan") and in central Asia.

#### Fumarole

Main article: [Fumarole](/wiki/Fumarole "Fumarole")

[Fumaroles](/wiki/Fumarole "Fumarole") are vents on the surface from which hot steam and volcanic gases erupt due to the presence of superheated groundwater, these may indicate volcanic activity. Fumaroles erupting sulfurous gases are also often called solfataras.[[48]](#cite_note-old-48)[[2]](#cite_note-dkp-2)

#### Geysers

Main article: [Geyser](/wiki/Geyser "Geyser")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Yellowstone_Castle_Geysir_Edit.jpg/250px-Yellowstone_Castle_Geysir_Edit.jpg)](/wiki/File:Yellowstone_Castle_Geysir_Edit.jpg)

Castle geyser eruption, [Yellowstone National Park](/wiki/Yellowstone_National_Park "Yellowstone National Park")

Geysers are springs which will occasionally erupt and discharge hot water and steam. Geysers may indicate ongoing magmatism, water underground is heated by hot rocks and [steam](/wiki/Steam "Steam") [pressure](/wiki/Vapor_pressure "Vapor pressure") builds up before being released along with a jet of hot water. Almost half of all active geysers are present in Yellowstone National Park, US.[[2]](#cite_note-dkp-2)[[49]](#cite_note-nps2-49)
