[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/Aerial_image_of_Stromboli_%28view_from_the_northeast%29.jpg/250px-Aerial_image_of_Stromboli_%28view_from_the_northeast%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aerial_image_of_Stromboli_(view_from_the_northeast).jpg)

The [Stromboli](/wiki/Stromboli "Stromboli") stratovolcano off the coast of [Sicily](/wiki/Sicily "Sicily") has erupted continuously for thousands of years, giving rise to its nickname "Lighthouse of the Mediterranean".

The material that is expelled in a [volcanic eruption](/wiki/Volcanic_eruption "Volcanic eruption") can be classified into three types:

1. [Volcanic gases](/wiki/Volcanic_gas "Volcanic gas"), a mixture made mostly of [steam](/wiki/Steam "Steam"), [carbon dioxide](/wiki/Carbon_dioxide "Carbon dioxide"), and a sulfur compound (either [sulfur dioxide](/wiki/Sulfur_dioxide "Sulfur dioxide"), SO2, or [hydrogen sulfide](/wiki/Hydrogen_sulfide "Hydrogen sulfide"), H2S, depending on the temperature)
2. [Lava](/wiki/Lava "Lava"), the name of magma when it emerges and flows over the surface
3. [Tephra](/wiki/Tephra "Tephra"), particles of solid material of all shapes and sizes ejected and thrown through the air[[51]](#cite_note-EB1911-51)[[52]](#cite_note-schmidt-1981-52)

### Volcanic gases

Main article: [Volcanic gas](/wiki/Volcanic_gas "Volcanic gas")

The concentrations of different [volcanic gases](/wiki/Volcanic_gas "Volcanic gas") can vary considerably from one volcano to the next. [Water vapour](/wiki/Water_vapour "Water vapour") is typically the most abundant volcanic gas, followed by [carbon dioxide](/wiki/Carbon_dioxide "Carbon dioxide")[[53]](#cite_note-53) and [sulfur dioxide](/wiki/Sulfur_dioxide "Sulfur dioxide"). Other principal volcanic gases include [hydrogen sulfide](/wiki/Hydrogen_sulfide "Hydrogen sulfide"), [hydrogen chloride](/wiki/Hydrogen_chloride "Hydrogen chloride"), and [hydrogen fluoride](/wiki/Hydrogen_fluoride "Hydrogen fluoride"). A large number of minor and trace gases are also found in volcanic emissions, for example [hydrogen](/wiki/Hydrogen "Hydrogen"), [carbon monoxide](/wiki/Carbon_monoxide "Carbon monoxide"), [halocarbons](/wiki/Halocarbon "Halocarbon"), organic compounds, and [volatile](/wiki/Volatile_(astrogeology) "Volatile (astrogeology)") metal chlorides.

### Lava flows

Main article: [Lava flow](/wiki/Lava_flow "Lava flow")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/Rinjani_1994_cropped.jpg/250px-Rinjani_1994_cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rinjani_1994_cropped.jpg)

[Mount Rinjani](/wiki/Mount_Rinjani "Mount Rinjani") eruption in 1994, in [Lombok](/wiki/Lombok "Lombok"), [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia")

The form and style of an eruption of a volcano is largely determined by the composition of the lava it erupts. The viscosity (how fluid the lava is) and the amount of dissolved gas are the most important characteristics of magma, and both are largely determined by the amount of silica in the magma. Magma rich in silica is much more viscous than silica-poor magma, and silica-rich magma also tends to contain more dissolved gases.

Lava can be broadly classified into four different compositions:[[54]](#cite_note-54)

* If the erupted [magma](/wiki/Magma "Magma") contains a high percentage (>63%) of [silica](/wiki/Silica "Silica"), the lava is described as *[felsic](/wiki/Felsic "Felsic")*. Felsic lavas ([dacites](/wiki/Dacite "Dacite") or [rhyolites](/wiki/Rhyolite "Rhyolite")) are highly [viscous](/wiki/Viscous "Viscous") and are erupted as domes or short, stubby flows.[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPhilpottsAgue200970–72-55) [Lassen Peak](/wiki/Lassen_Peak "Lassen Peak") in California is an example of a volcano formed from felsic lava and is actually a large lava dome.[[56]](#cite_note-56)

:   Because felsic magmas are so viscous, they tend to trap volatiles (gases) that are present, which leads to explosive volcanism. [Pyroclastic flows](/wiki/Pyroclastic_flow "Pyroclastic flow") ([ignimbrites](/wiki/Ignimbrite "Ignimbrite")) are highly hazardous products of such volcanoes since they hug the volcano's slopes and travel far from their vents during large eruptions. Temperatures as high as 850 °C (1,560 °F)[[57]](#cite_note-57) are known to occur in pyroclastic flows, which will incinerate everything flammable in their path, and thick layers of hot pyroclastic flow deposits can be laid down, often many meters thick.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPhilpottsAgue200973–77-58) [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska")'s [Valley of Ten Thousand Smokes](/wiki/Valley_of_Ten_Thousand_Smokes "Valley of Ten Thousand Smokes"), formed by the eruption of [Novarupta](/wiki/Novarupta "Novarupta") near [Katmai](/wiki/Mount_Katmai "Mount Katmai") in 1912, is an example of a thick pyroclastic flow or ignimbrite deposit.[[59]](#cite_note-59) Volcanic ash that is light enough to erupt high into the [Earth's atmosphere](/wiki/Earth%27s_atmosphere "Earth's atmosphere") as an [eruption column](/wiki/Eruption_column "Eruption column") may travel hundreds of kilometres before it falls back to ground as a fallout [tuff](/wiki/Tuff "Tuff"). Volcanic gases may remain in the [stratosphere](/wiki/Stratosphere "Stratosphere") for years.[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchmincke2003229-60)

:   Felsic magmas are formed within the crust, usually through the melting of crust rock from the heat of underlying mafic magmas. The lighter felsic magma floats on the mafic magma without significant mixing.[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPhilpottsAgue200915–16-61) Less commonly, felsic magmas are produced by extreme [fractional crystallization](/wiki/Fractional_crystallization_(geology) "Fractional crystallization (geology)") of more mafic magmas.[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPhilpottsAgue2009378-62) This is a process in which mafic minerals crystallize out of the slowly cooling magma, which enriches the remaining liquid in silica.

* If the erupted magma contains 52–63% silica, the lava is of *[intermediate composition](/wiki/Intermediate_composition "Intermediate composition")* or *[andesitic](/wiki/Andesitic "Andesitic")*. Intermediate magmas are characteristic of stratovolcanoes.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchmincke2003143-63) They are most commonly formed at [convergent boundaries](/wiki/Convergent_boundaries "Convergent boundaries") between [tectonic plates](/wiki/Tectonic_plate "Tectonic plate"), by several processes. One process is the hydration melting of mantle peridotite followed by fractional crystallization. Water from a subducting [slab](/wiki/Slab_(geology) "Slab (geology)") rises into the overlying mantle, lowering its melting point, particularly for the more silica-rich minerals. Fractional crystallization further enriches the magma in silica. It has also been suggested that intermediate magmas are produced by the melting of sediments carried downwards by the subducted slab.[[64]](#cite_note-64) Another process is magma mixing between felsic rhyolitic and mafic basaltic magmas in an intermediate reservoir before emplacement or lava flow.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPhilpottsAgue2009377-65)
* If the erupted magma contains <52% and >45% silica, the lava is called *[mafic](/wiki/Mafic "Mafic")* (because it contains higher percentages of [magnesium](/wiki/Magnesium "Magnesium") (Mg) and iron (Fe)) or [basaltic](/wiki/Basalt "Basalt"). These lavas are usually hotter and much less viscous than felsic lavas. Mafic magmas are formed by partial melting of the dry mantle, with limited fractional crystallization and assimilation of crustal material.[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPhilpottsAgue200916-66)

:   Mafic lavas occur in a wide range of settings. These include [mid-ocean ridges](/wiki/Mid-ocean_ridge "Mid-ocean ridge"); [Shield volcanoes](/wiki/Shield_volcanoes "Shield volcanoes") (such the [Hawaiian Islands](/wiki/Hawaiian_Islands "Hawaiian Islands"), including [Mauna Loa](/wiki/Mauna_Loa "Mauna Loa") and [Kilauea](/wiki/Kilauea "Kilauea")), on both [oceanic](/wiki/Oceanic_crust "Oceanic crust") and [continental crust](/wiki/Continental_crust "Continental crust"); and as continental [flood basalts](/wiki/Flood_basalt "Flood basalt").

* Some erupted magmas contain ≤45% silica and produce *[ultramafic](/wiki/Ultramafic "Ultramafic")* lava. Ultramafic flows, also known as [komatiites](/wiki/Komatiite "Komatiite"), are very rare; indeed, very few have been erupted at Earth's surface since the [Proterozoic](/wiki/Proterozoic "Proterozoic"), when the planet's heat flow was higher. They are (or were) the hottest lavas, and were probably more fluid than common mafic lavas, with a viscosity less than a tenth that of hot basalt magma.[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPhilpottsAgue200924-67)

Mafic lava flows show two varieties of surface texture: ʻAʻa (pronounced [[ˈʔaʔa]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Hawaiian "Help:IPA/Hawaiian")) and [pāhoehoe](/wiki/P%C4%81hoehoe "Pāhoehoe") ([[paːˈho.eˈho.e]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Hawaiian "Help:IPA/Hawaiian")), both [Hawaiian](/wiki/Hawaiian_language "Hawaiian language") words. ʻAʻa is characterized by a rough, clinkery surface and is the typical texture of cooler basalt lava flows. Pāhoehoe is characterized by its smooth and often ropey or wrinkly surface and is generally formed from more fluid lava flows. Pāhoehoe flows are sometimes observed to transition to ʻaʻa flows as they move away from the vent, but never the reverse.[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchmincke2003131–132-68)

More silicic lava flows take the form of block lava, where the flow is covered with angular, vesicle-poor blocks. [Rhyolitic](/wiki/Rhyolitic "Rhyolitic") flows typically consist largely of [obsidian](/wiki/Obsidian "Obsidian").[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchmincke2003132-69)

### Tephra

Main article: [Tephra](/wiki/Tephra "Tephra")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/Tuff_shards.jpg/250px-Tuff_shards.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tuff_shards.jpg)

Light-microscope image of [tuff](/wiki/Tuff "Tuff") as seen in [thin section](/wiki/Thin_section "Thin section") (long dimension is several mm): the curved shapes of altered glass shards (ash fragments) are well preserved, although the glass is partly altered. The shapes were formed around bubbles of expanding, water-rich gas.

Tephra is made when magma inside the volcano is blown apart by the rapid expansion of hot volcanic gases. Magma commonly explodes as the gas dissolved in it comes out of solution as the pressure decreases [when it flows to the surface](/wiki/Extrusion "Extrusion"). These violent explosions produce particles of material that can then fly from the volcano. Solid particles smaller than 2 mm in diameter ([sand-sized](/wiki/Sand "Sand") or smaller) are called volcanic ash.[[51]](#cite_note-EB1911-51)[[52]](#cite_note-schmidt-1981-52)

Tephra and other [volcaniclastics](/wiki/Volcaniclastics "Volcaniclastics") (shattered volcanic material) make up more of the volume of many volcanoes than do lava flows. Volcaniclastics may have contributed as much as a third of all sedimentation in the geologic record. The production of large volumes of tephra is characteristic of explosive volcanism.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFisherSchmincke198489-70)

### Dissection

Through natural processes, mainly [erosion](/wiki/Erosion "Erosion"), so much of the solidified erupted material that makes up the mantle of a volcano may be stripped away that its inner anatomy becomes apparent. Using the metaphor of [biological anatomy](/wiki/Anatomy "Anatomy"), such a process is called "dissection".[[71]](#cite_note-71) When the volcano is extinct, a plug forms on its vent, over time due to erosion, the volcanic cone slowly erodes away leaving the resistant lava plug intact.[[2]](#cite_note-dkp-2) [Cinder Hill](/wiki/Cinder_Hill "Cinder Hill"), a feature of [Mount Bird](/wiki/Mount_Bird "Mount Bird") on [Ross Island](/wiki/Ross_Island "Ross Island"), [Antarctica](/wiki/Antarctica "Antarctica"), is a prominent example of a dissected volcano. Volcanoes that were, on a geological timescale, recently active, such as for example [Mount Kaimon](/wiki/Mount_Kaimon "Mount Kaimon") in southern [Kyūshū](/wiki/Ky%C5%ABsh%C5%AB "Kyūshū"), [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"), tend to be undissected. [Devils Tower](/wiki/Devils_Tower "Devils Tower") in Wyoming is a famous example of exposed volcanic plug.

## Volcanic eruptions

Main article: [Volcanic eruption](/wiki/Volcanic_eruption "Volcanic eruption")

As of December 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Volcano&action=edit), the [Smithsonian Institution](/wiki/Smithsonian_Institution "Smithsonian Institution")'s [Global Volcanism Program](/wiki/Global_Volcanism_Program "Global Volcanism Program") database of volcanic eruptions in the [Holocene](/wiki/Holocene "Holocene") [Epoch](/wiki/Epoch_(geology) "Epoch (geology)") (the last 11,700 years) lists 9,901 confirmed eruptions from 859 volcanoes. The database also lists 1,113 uncertain eruptions and 168 discredited eruptions for the same time interval.[[72]](#cite_note-GVP501-72)[[73]](#cite_note-Volc_Count-73)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Volcanic_injection.svg/250px-Volcanic_injection.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Volcanic_injection.svg)

Schematic of volcano injection of aerosols and gases

Eruption styles are broadly divided into magmatic, phreatomagmatic (hydrovolcanic), and phreatic eruptions.[[74]](#cite_note-vol_cash-74) The intensity of explosive volcanism is expressed using the [volcanic explosivity index](/wiki/Volcanic_explosivity_index "Volcanic explosivity index") (VEI), which ranges from 0 for Hawaiian-type eruptions to 8 for supervolcanic eruptions:[[75]](#cite_note-75)[[76]](#cite_note-nps3-76)

* Magmatic eruptions are driven primarily by gas release due to decompression.[[74]](#cite_note-vol_cash-74) Low-viscosity magma with little dissolved gas produces relatively gentle effusive eruptions. High-viscosity magma with a high content of dissolved gas produces violent [explosive eruptions](/wiki/Explosive_eruption "Explosive eruption"). The range of observed eruption styles is expressed from historical examples.
* [Hawaiian eruptions](/wiki/Hawaiian_eruption "Hawaiian eruption") are typical of volcanoes that erupt mafic lava with a relatively low gas content. These are almost entirely effusive, producing local [lava fountains](/wiki/Lava_fountain "Lava fountain") and highly fluid lava flows but relatively little tephra. They are named after the [Hawaiian volcanoes](/wiki/Hawaii_hotspot "Hawaii hotspot"). The eruption column from these eruptions does not exceed 2 kilometres (1.2 mi) in height.
* [Strombolian eruptions](/wiki/Strombolian_eruption "Strombolian eruption") are characterized by moderate viscosities and dissolved gas levels. They are characterized by frequent but short-lived eruptions that can produce eruptive columns hundreds of meters high, which can also be seen in a [gas slug](/wiki/Gas_slug "Gas slug"). Their primary product is [scoria](/wiki/Scoria "Scoria"). They are named after [Stromboli](/wiki/Stromboli "Stromboli").
* [Vulcanian eruptions](/wiki/Vulcanian_eruption "Vulcanian eruption") are characterized by yet higher viscosities and partial crystallization of magma, which is often intermediate in composition. Eruptions take the form of short-lived explosions for several hours, which destroy a central dome and eject large lava blocks and bombs. This is followed by an effusive phase that rebuilds the central dome. Vulcanian eruptions are named after [Vulcano](/wiki/Vulcano "Vulcano"). Eruption columns from these eruptions do not exceed 20 kilometres (12 mi) in height.
* [Peléan eruptions](/wiki/Pel%C3%A9an_eruption "Peléan eruption") are more violent still, being characterized by dome growth and collapse that produces various kinds of pyroclastic flows. They are named after [Mount Pelée](/wiki/Mount_Pel%C3%A9e "Mount Pelée").
* [Plinian eruptions](/wiki/Plinian_eruption "Plinian eruption") are characterized by sustained huge eruption columns whose collapse produces catastrophic pyroclastic flows. They are named after [Pliny the Younger](/wiki/Pliny_the_Younger "Pliny the Younger"), who chronicled the Plinian [eruption of Mount Vesuvius in 79](/wiki/Eruption_of_Mount_Vesuvius_in_79 "Eruption of Mount Vesuvius in 79") AD.
* Ultra-Plinian eruptions are the largest of all volcanic eruptions are more intense, have a higher eruption rate than Plinian ones, form higher eruption columns and may form large calderas. These eruptions produce rhyolitic lava, tephra, [pumice](/wiki/Pumice "Pumice") and thick pyroclastic flows that cover vast areas and may produce widespread [ash-fall](/wiki/Volcanic_ash "Volcanic ash") deposits. Examples are [Mt. Mazama](/wiki/Mount_Mazama "Mount Mazama") and Yellowstone.
* [Phreatomagmatic eruptions](/wiki/Phreatomagmatic_eruption "Phreatomagmatic eruption") (hydrovolcanic) are characterized by interaction of rising magma with [groundwater](/wiki/Groundwater "Groundwater"). They are driven by the resulting rapid buildup of pressure in the [superheated](/wiki/Superheating "Superheating") groundwater.
* [Phreatic eruptions](/wiki/Phreatic_eruption "Phreatic eruption") are characterized by superheating of groundwater that comes in contact with hot rock or magma. They are distinguished from phreatomagmatic eruptions because the erupted material is all [country rock](/wiki/Country_rock_(geology) "Country rock (geology)"); no magma is erupted.

## Volcanic activity

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Pompeii_-_Casa_del_Centenario_-_MAN.jpg/250px-Pompeii_-_Casa_del_Centenario_-_MAN.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pompeii_-_Casa_del_Centenario_-_MAN.jpg)

[Fresco](/wiki/Fresco "Fresco") with [Mount Vesuvius](/wiki/Mount_Vesuvius "Mount Vesuvius") behind [Bacchus](/wiki/Bacchus "Bacchus") and [Agathodaemon](/wiki/Agathodaemon "Agathodaemon"), as seen in [Pompeii](/wiki/Pompeii "Pompeii")'s [House of the Centenary](/wiki/House_of_the_Centenary "House of the Centenary")

Volcanoes vary greatly in their level of activity, with individual volcanic systems having an *eruption recurrence* ranging from several times a year to once in tens of thousands of years.[[77]](#cite_note-77) Volcanoes are informally described as **erupting**, **active**, **dormant**, or **extinct**, but the definitions of these terms are not entirely uniform among volcanologists. The level of activity of most volcanoes falls upon a graduated spectrum, with much overlap between categories, and does not always fit neatly into only one of these three separate categories.[[6]](#cite_note-ade-6)

### Erupting

The [USGS](/wiki/USGS "USGS") defines a volcano as "erupting" whenever the ejection of magma from any point on the volcano is visible, including visible magma still contained within the walls of the summit crater.

### Active

Main article: [Active volcano](/wiki/Active_volcano "Active volcano")

While there is no international consensus among volcanologists on how to define an active volcano, the USGS defines a volcano as *active* whenever subterranean indicators, such as [earthquake swarms](/wiki/Earthquake_swarm "Earthquake swarm"), ground inflation, or unusually high levels of carbon dioxide or sulfur dioxide are present.[[78]](#cite_note-78)[[79]](#cite_note-activity_levels-79)

### Dormant and reactivated

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Narcondam_island.jpg/250px-Narcondam_island.jpg)](/wiki/File:Narcondam_island.jpg)

[Narcondam Island](/wiki/Narcondam_Island "Narcondam Island"), India, is classified as a dormant volcano by the [Geological Survey of India](/wiki/Geological_Survey_of_India "Geological Survey of India").

The USGS defines a dormant volcano as any volcano that is not showing any signs of unrest such as earthquake swarms, ground swelling, or excessive noxious gas emissions, but which shows signs that it could yet become active again.[[79]](#cite_note-activity_levels-79) Many dormant volcanoes have not erupted for thousands of years, but have still shown signs that they may be likely to erupt again in the future.[[80]](#cite_note-Nelson2016-80)[[81]](#cite_note-VolcWorldDormant-81) Technically, any volcano that is dormant is also considered to be geologically "active".[[5]](#cite_note-www.usgs.gov2-5)

In an article justifying the re-classification of Alaska's [Mount Edgecumbe](/wiki/Mount_Edgecumbe_(Alaska) "Mount Edgecumbe (Alaska)") volcano from "dormant" to "active", volcanologists at the [Alaska Volcano Observatory](/wiki/Alaska_Volcano_Observatory "Alaska Volcano Observatory") pointed out that the term "dormant" in reference to volcanoes has been deprecated over the past few decades and that "[t]he term "dormant volcano" is so little used and undefined in modern volcanology that the Encyclopedia of Volcanoes (2000) does not contain it in the glossaries or index",[[82]](#cite_note-82) however the USGS still widely employs the term.

Previously a volcano was often considered to be extinct if there were no written records of its activity. Such a generalization is inconsistent with observation and deeper study, as has occurred recently with the unexpected eruption of the [Chaitén volcano](/wiki/Chait%C3%A9n_(volcano) "Chaitén (volcano)") in 2008.[[83]](#cite_note-Castro2009-83) Modern volcanic activity monitoring techniques, and improvements in the modelling of the factors that produce eruptions, have helped the understanding of why volcanoes may remain dormant for a long time, and then become unexpectedly active again. The potential for eruptions, and their style, depend mainly upon the state of the magma storage system under the volcano, the eruption trigger mechanism and its timescale.[[84]](#cite_note-Cserép2023-84): 95 For example, the [Yellowstone](/wiki/Yellowstone_Caldera "Yellowstone Caldera") volcano has a repose/recharge period of around 700,000 years, and [Toba](/wiki/Toba_Lake "Toba Lake") of around 380,000 years.[[85]](#cite_note-chesner1991-85) [Vesuvius](/wiki/Vesuvius "Vesuvius") was described by Roman writers as having been covered with gardens and vineyards before its unexpected [eruption of 79 CE](/wiki/Eruption_of_Mount_Vesuvius_in_79 "Eruption of Mount Vesuvius in 79"), which destroyed the towns of [Herculaneum](/wiki/Herculaneum "Herculaneum") and [Pompeii](/wiki/Pompeii "Pompeii").

Accordingly, it can sometimes be difficult to distinguish between an extinct volcano and a dormant (inactive) one. Long volcano dormancy is known to decrease awareness.[[84]](#cite_note-Cserép2023-84): 96 [Pinatubo](/wiki/Pinatubo "Pinatubo") was an inconspicuous volcano, unknown to most people in the surrounding areas, and initially not seismically monitored before its unanticipated and catastrophic eruption of 1991. Two other examples of volcanoes that were once thought to be extinct, before springing back into eruptive activity were the long-dormant [Soufrière Hills](/wiki/Soufri%C3%A8re_Hills "Soufrière Hills") volcano on the island of [Montserrat](/wiki/Montserrat "Montserrat"), thought to be extinct until activity resumed in 1995 (turning its capital [Plymouth](/wiki/Plymouth,_Montserrat "Plymouth, Montserrat") into a [ghost town](/wiki/Ghost_town "Ghost town")) and [Fourpeaked Mountain](/wiki/Fourpeaked_Mountain "Fourpeaked Mountain") in Alaska, which, before its September 2006 eruption, had not erupted since before 8000 BCE. Another example is the [Taftan volcano](/wiki/Taftan_(volcano) "Taftan (volcano)") in southwestern Iran. This volcano was long thought by volcanologists to be extinct, with its last eruption having occurred an estimated 710,000 years ago. However, beginning around June 2023, the volcano began experiencing uplifting near its summit, suggesting that the volcano was dormant.[[86]](#cite_note-Iranian_volcano-86)

### Extinct

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/Capulin_1980_tde00005.jpg/250px-Capulin_1980_tde00005.jpg)](/wiki/File:Capulin_1980_tde00005.jpg)

[Capulin Volcano National Monument](/wiki/Capulin_Volcano_National_Monument "Capulin Volcano National Monument") in New Mexico, US

Extinct volcanoes are those that scientists consider unlikely to erupt again because the volcano no longer has a magma supply. Examples of extinct volcanoes are many volcanoes on the [Hawaiian–Emperor seamount chain](/wiki/Hawaiian%E2%80%93Emperor_seamount_chain "Hawaiian–Emperor seamount chain") in the Pacific Ocean (although some volcanoes at the eastern end of the chain are active), [Hohentwiel](/wiki/Hohentwiel "Hohentwiel") in [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"), [Shiprock](/wiki/Shiprock "Shiprock") in [New Mexico](/wiki/New_Mexico "New Mexico"), U.S., [Capulin](/wiki/Capulin_Volcano_National_Monument "Capulin Volcano National Monument") in New Mexico, U.S., [Zuidwal volcano](/wiki/Zuidwal_volcano "Zuidwal volcano") in the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands"), and many volcanoes in [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy") such as [Monte Vulture](/wiki/Monte_Vulture "Monte Vulture"). [Edinburgh Castle](/wiki/Edinburgh_Castle "Edinburgh Castle") in Scotland is located atop an extinct volcano, which forms [Castle Rock](/wiki/Castle_Rock_(Edinburgh) "Castle Rock (Edinburgh)"). Whether a volcano is truly extinct is often difficult to determine. Since "supervolcano" [calderas](/wiki/Caldera "Caldera") can have eruptive lifespans sometimes measured in millions of years, a caldera that has not produced an eruption in tens of thousands of years may be considered dormant instead of extinct. An individual volcano in a monogenetic volcanic field can be extinct, but that does not mean a completely new volcano might not erupt close by with little or no warning, as its field may have an active magma supply.

### Volcanic-alert level

The three common popular classifications of volcanoes can be subjective and some volcanoes thought to have been extinct have erupted again. To help prevent people from falsely believing they are not at risk when living on or near a volcano, countries have adopted new classifications to describe the various levels and stages of volcanic activity.[[87]](#cite_note-87) Some alert systems use different numbers or colours to designate the different stages. Other systems use colours and words. Some systems use a combination of both.

## Decade volcanoes

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/Koryaksky_volcano_Petropavlovsk-Kamchatsky_oct-2005.jpg/250px-Koryaksky_volcano_Petropavlovsk-Kamchatsky_oct-2005.jpg)](/wiki/File:Koryaksky_volcano_Petropavlovsk-Kamchatsky_oct-2005.jpg)

[Koryaksky](/wiki/Koryaksky "Koryaksky") volcano towering over [Petropavlovsk-Kamchatsky](/wiki/Petropavlovsk-Kamchatsky "Petropavlovsk-Kamchatsky") on [Kamchatka Peninsula](/wiki/Kamchatka_Peninsula "Kamchatka Peninsula"), Far Eastern [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia")

Main articles: [Lists of volcanoes](/wiki/Lists_of_volcanoes "Lists of volcanoes") and [Decade Volcanoes](/wiki/Decade_Volcanoes "Decade Volcanoes")

The Decade Volcanoes are 16 volcanoes identified by the [International Association of Volcanology and Chemistry of the Earth's Interior](/wiki/International_Association_of_Volcanology_and_Chemistry_of_the_Earth%27s_Interior "International Association of Volcanology and Chemistry of the Earth's Interior") (IAVCEI) as being worthy of particular study in light of their history of large, destructive eruptions and proximity to populated areas. They are named Decade Volcanoes because the project was initiated as part of the United Nations-sponsored [International Decade for Natural Disaster Reduction](/wiki/International_Decade_for_Natural_Disaster_Reduction "International Decade for Natural Disaster Reduction") (the 1990s). The 16 current Decade Volcanoes are:

* [Avachinsky](/wiki/Avachinsky "Avachinsky")-[Koryaksky](/wiki/Koryaksky "Koryaksky") (grouped together), [Kamchatka](/wiki/Kamchatka "Kamchatka"), Russia
* [Nevado de Colima](/wiki/Nevado_de_Colima "Nevado de Colima"), [Jalisco](/wiki/Jalisco "Jalisco") and [Colima](/wiki/Colima "Colima"), Mexico
* [Mount Etna](/wiki/Mount_Etna "Mount Etna"), Sicily, Italy
* [Galeras](/wiki/Galeras "Galeras"), [Nariño](/wiki/Nari%C3%B1o "Nariño"), Colombia
* [Mauna Loa](/wiki/Mauna_Loa "Mauna Loa"), Hawaii, US
* [Mount Merapi](/wiki/Mount_Merapi "Mount Merapi"), [Central Java](/wiki/Central_Java "Central Java"), Indonesia
* [Mount Nyiragongo](/wiki/Mount_Nyiragongo "Mount Nyiragongo"), Democratic Republic of the Congo
* [Mount Rainier](/wiki/Mount_Rainier "Mount Rainier"), [Washington](/wiki/Washington_(state) "Washington (state)"), US
* [Sakurajima](/wiki/Sakurajima "Sakurajima"), [Kagoshima Prefecture](/wiki/Kagoshima_Prefecture "Kagoshima Prefecture"), Japan
* [Santa Maria/Santiaguito](/wiki/Santa_Mar%C3%ADa_(volcano) "Santa María (volcano)"), Guatemala
* [Santorini](/wiki/Santorini "Santorini"), [Cyclades](/wiki/Cyclades "Cyclades"), Greece
* [Taal Volcano](/wiki/Taal_Volcano "Taal Volcano"), [Luzon](/wiki/Luzon "Luzon"), Philippines
* [Teide](/wiki/Teide "Teide"), Canary Islands, Spain
* [Ulawun](/wiki/Ulawun "Ulawun"), [New Britain](/wiki/New_Britain "New Britain"), Papua New Guinea
* [Mount Unzen](/wiki/Mount_Unzen "Mount Unzen"), [Nagasaki Prefecture](/wiki/Nagasaki_Prefecture "Nagasaki Prefecture"), Japan
* [Vesuvius](/wiki/Vesuvius "Vesuvius"), [Naples](/wiki/Province_of_Naples "Province of Naples"), Italy

The [Deep Earth Carbon Degassing Project](/wiki/Deep_Earth_Carbon_Degassing_Project "Deep Earth Carbon Degassing Project"), an initiative of the [Deep Carbon Observatory](/wiki/Deep_Carbon_Observatory "Deep Carbon Observatory"), monitors nine volcanoes, two of which are Decade volcanoes. The focus of the Deep Earth Carbon Degassing Project is to use [Multi-Component Gas Analyzer System](/wiki/Multi-Component_Gas_Analyzer_System "Multi-Component Gas Analyzer System") instruments to measure CO2/SO2 ratios in real-time and in high-resolution to allow detection of the pre-eruptive degassing of rising magmas, improving [prediction of volcanic activity](/wiki/Prediction_of_volcanic_activity "Prediction of volcanic activity").[[88]](#cite_note-88)

## Volcanoes and humans

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/Mauna_Loa_atmospheric_transmission.png/250px-Mauna_Loa_atmospheric_transmission.png)](/wiki/File:Mauna_Loa_atmospheric_transmission.png)

Solar radiation graph 1958–2008, showing how the radiation is reduced after major volcanic eruptions

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/SO2_Galapagos_20051101.jpg/250px-SO2_Galapagos_20051101.jpg)](/wiki/File:SO2_Galapagos_20051101.jpg)

[Sulfur dioxide](/wiki/Sulfur_dioxide "Sulfur dioxide") concentration over the [Sierra Negra Volcano](/wiki/Sierra_Negra_(Gal%C3%A1pagos) "Sierra Negra (Galápagos)"), [Galapagos Islands](/wiki/Galapagos_Islands "Galapagos Islands"), during an eruption in October 2005

Volcanic eruptions pose a significant threat to human civilization. However, volcanic activity has also provided humans with important resources.

### Hazards

Main article: [Volcanic hazard](/wiki/Volcanic_hazard "Volcanic hazard")

There are many different [types of volcanic eruptions](/wiki/Types_of_volcanic_eruptions "Types of volcanic eruptions") and associated activity: [phreatic eruptions](/wiki/Phreatic_eruptions "Phreatic eruptions") (steam-generated eruptions), explosive eruptions of high-[silica](/wiki/Silica "Silica") lava (e.g., [rhyolite](/wiki/Rhyolite "Rhyolite")), effusive eruptions of low-silica lava (e.g., [basalt](/wiki/Basalt "Basalt")), [sector collapses](/wiki/Sector_collapse "Sector collapse"), [pyroclastic flows](/wiki/Pyroclastic_flow "Pyroclastic flow"), [lahars](/wiki/Lahar "Lahar") (debris flows) and [volcanic gas emissions](/wiki/Volcanic_gas "Volcanic gas"). These can pose a hazard to humans. Earthquakes, [hot springs](/wiki/Hot_spring "Hot spring"), [fumaroles](/wiki/Fumarole "Fumarole"), [mud pots](/wiki/Mud_pot "Mud pot") and [geysers](/wiki/Geyser "Geyser") often accompany volcanic activity.

Volcanic gases can reach the stratosphere, where they form [sulfuric acid](/wiki/Sulfuric_acid "Sulfuric acid") aerosols that can reflect solar radiation and lower surface temperatures significantly.[[89]](#cite_note-89) Sulfur dioxide from the eruption of [Huaynaputina](/wiki/Huaynaputina "Huaynaputina") may have caused the [Russian famine of 1601–1603](/wiki/Russian_famine_of_1601%E2%80%931603 "Russian famine of 1601–1603").[[90]](#cite_note-90) Chemical reactions of sulfate aerosols in the stratosphere can also damage the [ozone layer](/wiki/Ozone_layer "Ozone layer"), and acids such as [hydrogen chloride](/wiki/Hydrogen_chloride "Hydrogen chloride") (HCl) and hydrogen fluoride (HF) can fall to the ground as [acid rain](/wiki/Acid_rain "Acid rain"). Excessive fluoride salts from eruptions have poisoned [livestock](/wiki/Livestock "Livestock") in Iceland on multiple occasions.[[91]](#cite_note-91): 39–58 [Explosive volcanic eruptions](/wiki/Explosive_eruption "Explosive eruption") release the greenhouse gas [carbon dioxide](/wiki/Carbon_dioxide "Carbon dioxide") and thus provide a deep source of [carbon](/wiki/Carbon "Carbon") for [biogeochemical cycles](/wiki/Biogeochemical_cycle "Biogeochemical cycle").[[92]](#cite_note-92)

Ash thrown into the air by eruptions can present a hazard to aircraft, especially [jet aircraft](/wiki/Jet_aircraft "Jet aircraft") where the particles can be melted by the high operating temperature; the melted particles then adhere to the [turbine](/wiki/Turbine "Turbine") blades and alter their shape, disrupting the operation of the turbine. This can cause major disruptions to air travel.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/Large_eruptions.jpg/330px-Large_eruptions.jpg)](/wiki/File:Large_eruptions.jpg)

Comparison of major United States prehistoric eruptions ([VEI 7 and 8](/wiki/VEI "VEI")) with major historical volcanic eruptions in the 19th and 20th century (VEI 5, 6 and 7). From left to right: Yellowstone 2.1 Ma, Yellowstone 1.3 Ma, Long Valley 6.26 Ma, Yellowstone 0.64 Ma . 19th century eruptions: Tambora 1815, Krakatoa 1883. 20th century eruptions: Novarupta 1912, St. Helens 1980, Pinatubo 1991.

A [volcanic winter](/wiki/Volcanic_winter "Volcanic winter") is thought to have taken place around 70,000 years ago after the [supereruption](/wiki/Supereruption "Supereruption") of [Lake Toba](/wiki/Lake_Toba "Lake Toba") on Sumatra island in Indonesia.[[93]](#cite_note-93) This may have created a [population bottleneck](/wiki/Toba_catastrophe_theory "Toba catastrophe theory") that affected the genetic inheritance of all humans today.[[94]](#cite_note-94) Volcanic eruptions may have contributed to major extinction events, such as the [End-Ordovician](/wiki/Ordovician-Silurian_extinction_events "Ordovician-Silurian extinction events"), [Permian-Triassic](/wiki/Permian-Triassic "Permian-Triassic"), and [Late Devonian](/wiki/Late_Devonian_extinction "Late Devonian extinction") [mass extinctions](/wiki/Mass_extinction "Mass extinction").[[95]](#cite_note-95)

The 1815 eruption of [Mount Tambora](/wiki/Mount_Tambora "Mount Tambora") created global climate anomalies that became known as the "[Year Without a Summer](/wiki/Year_Without_a_Summer "Year Without a Summer")" because of the effect on North American and European weather.[[96]](#cite_note-96) The freezing winter of 1740–41, which led to widespread [famine](/wiki/Irish_Famine_(1740%E2%80%931741) "Irish Famine (1740–1741)") in northern Europe, may also owe its origins to a volcanic eruption.[[97]](#cite_note-97)

### Benefits

See also: [Volcanogenic massive sulfide ore deposit](/wiki/Volcanogenic_massive_sulfide_ore_deposit "Volcanogenic massive sulfide ore deposit") and [Geothermal power](/wiki/Geothermal_power "Geothermal power")

Although volcanic eruptions pose considerable hazards to humans, past volcanic activity has created important economic resources. Tuff formed from volcanic ash is a relatively soft rock, and it has been used for construction since ancient times.[[98]](#cite_note-marcari-etal-2007-98)[[99]](#cite_note-dolan-etal-2019-99) The Romans often used tuff, which is abundant in Italy, for construction.[[100]](#cite_note-jackson-etal-2005-100) The [Rapa Nui](/wiki/Rapa_Nui "Rapa Nui") people used tuff to make most of the *[moai](/wiki/Moai "Moai")* statues in [Easter Island](/wiki/Easter_Island "Easter Island").[[101]](#cite_note-collins-2016-150-151-101)

Volcanic ash and weathered basalt produce some of the most fertile soil in the world, rich in nutrients such as iron, magnesium, potassium, calcium, and phosphorus.[[102]](#cite_note-kiprop-2019-102) Volcanic activity is responsible for emplacing valuable mineral resources, such as metal ores.[[102]](#cite_note-kiprop-2019-102) It is accompanied by high rates of heat flow from Earth's interior. These can be tapped as [geothermal power](/wiki/Geothermal_power "Geothermal power").[[102]](#cite_note-kiprop-2019-102)

Tourism associated with volcanoes is also a worldwide industry.[[103]](#cite_note-Thomaidis2021-103)

### Safety considerations

Many volcanoes near human settlements are heavily monitored with the aim of providing adequate advance warnings of imminent eruptions to nearby populations. Also, a better modern-day understanding of volcanology has led to some better informed governmental and public responses to unanticipated volcanic activities. While the science of volcanology may not yet be capable of predicting the exact times and dates of eruptions far into the future, on suitably monitored volcanoes the monitoring of ongoing volcanic indicators is often capable of predicting imminent eruptions with advance warnings minimally of hours, and usually of days prior to any eruptions.[[104]](#cite_note-104) The diversity of volcanoes and their complexities mean that eruption forecasts for the foreseeable future will be based on [probability](/wiki/Probability_theory "Probability theory"), and the application of [risk management](/wiki/Risk_management "Risk management"). Even then, some eruptions will have no useful warning. An example of this occurred in March 2017, when a tourist group was witnessing a presumed to be predictable Mount Etna eruption and the flowing lava came in contact with a snow accumulation causing a situational phreatic explosion causing injury to ten persons.[[103]](#cite_note-Thomaidis2021-103) Other types of significant eruptions are known to give useful warnings of only hours at the most by seismic monitoring.[[83]](#cite_note-Castro2009-83) The recent demonstration of a magma chamber with repose times of tens of thousands of years, with potential for rapid recharge so potentially decreasing warning times, under the youngest volcano in central Europe,[[84]](#cite_note-Cserép2023-84) does not tell us if more careful monitoring will be useful.

Scientists are known to perceive risk, with its social elements, differently from local populations and those that undertake social risk assessments on their behalf, so that both disruptive false alarms and retrospective blame, when disasters occur, will continue to happen.[[105]](#cite_note-105): 1–3

Thus in many cases, while volcanic eruptions may still cause major property destruction, the periodic large-scale loss of human life that was once associated with many volcanic eruptions, has recently been significantly reduced in areas where volcanoes are adequately monitored. This life-saving ability is derived via such volcanic-activity monitoring programs, through the greater abilities of local officials to facilitate timely evacuations based upon the greater modern-day knowledge of volcanism that is now available, and upon improved communications technologies such as cell phones. Such operations tend to provide enough time for humans to escape at least with their lives before a pending eruption. One example of such a recent successful volcanic evacuation was the [Mount Pinatubo](/wiki/Mount_Pinatubo "Mount Pinatubo") evacuation of 1991. This evacuation is believed to have saved 20,000 lives.[[106]](#cite_note-106) In the case of [Mount Etna](/wiki/Mount_Etna "Mount Etna"), a 2021 review found 77 deaths due to eruptions since 1536 but none since 1987.[[103]](#cite_note-Thomaidis2021-103)

Citizens who may be concerned about their own exposure to risk from nearby volcanic activity should familiarize themselves with the types of, and quality of, volcano monitoring and public notification procedures being employed by governmental authorities in their areas.[[107]](#cite_note-107)

## Volcanoes on other celestial bodies

See also: [List of extraterrestrial volcanoes](/wiki/List_of_extraterrestrial_volcanoes "List of extraterrestrial volcanoes"), [Volcanism on the Moon](/wiki/Volcanism_on_the_Moon "Volcanism on the Moon"), [Volcanism on Mars](/wiki/Volcanism_on_Mars "Volcanism on Mars"), [Volcanism on Io](/wiki/Volcanism_on_Io "Volcanism on Io"), and [Volcanism on Venus](/wiki/Volcanism_on_Venus "Volcanism on Venus")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Tvashtarvideo.gif/250px-Tvashtarvideo.gif)](/wiki/File:Tvashtarvideo.gif)

The [Tvashtar](/wiki/Tvashtar_Paterae "Tvashtar Paterae") volcano erupts a plume 330 km (205 mi) above the surface of [Jupiter](/wiki/Jupiter "Jupiter")'s moon [Io](/wiki/Io_(moon) "Io (moon)").

Earth's [Moon](/wiki/Moon "Moon") has no large volcanoes and no current volcanic activity, although recent evidence suggests it may still possess a partially molten core.[[108]](#cite_note-108) However, the Moon does have many volcanic features such as [maria](/wiki/Lunar_mare "Lunar mare")[[109]](#cite_note-109) (the darker patches seen on the Moon), [rilles](/wiki/Rille "Rille")[[110]](#cite_note-110) and [domes](/wiki/Lunar_dome "Lunar dome").[[111]](#cite_note-111)

The planet [Venus](/wiki/Venus "Venus") has a surface that is 90% [basalt](/wiki/Basalt "Basalt"), indicating that volcanism played a major role in shaping its surface. The planet may have had a major global resurfacing event about 500 million years ago,[[112]](#cite_note-112) from what scientists can tell from the density of impact craters on the surface. [Lava flows](/wiki/Lava_flows "Lava flows") are widespread and forms of volcanism not present on Earth occur as well. Changes in the planet's atmosphere and observations of lightning have been attributed to ongoing volcanic eruptions, although there is no confirmation of whether or not Venus is still volcanically active. However, radar sounding by the Magellan probe revealed evidence for comparatively recent volcanic activity at Venus's highest volcano [Maat Mons](/wiki/Maat_Mons "Maat Mons"), in the form of [ash flows](/wiki/Ash_flow "Ash flow") near the summit and on the northern flank.[[113]](#cite_note-113) However, the interpretation of the flows as ash flows has been questioned.[[114]](#cite_note-114)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Olympus_Mons_alt.jpg/250px-Olympus_Mons_alt.jpg)](/wiki/File:Olympus_Mons_alt.jpg)

[Olympus Mons](/wiki/Olympus_Mons "Olympus Mons") ([Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin"), "Mount Olympus"), located on the planet Mars, is the tallest known mountain in the [Solar System](/wiki/Solar_System "Solar System").

There are several extinct volcanoes on [Mars](/wiki/Mars "Mars"), four of which are vast shield volcanoes far bigger than any on Earth. They include [Arsia Mons](/wiki/Arsia_Mons "Arsia Mons"), [Ascraeus Mons](/wiki/Ascraeus_Mons "Ascraeus Mons"), [Hecates Tholus](/wiki/Hecates_Tholus "Hecates Tholus"), [Olympus Mons](/wiki/Olympus_Mons "Olympus Mons"), and [Pavonis Mons](/wiki/Pavonis_Mons "Pavonis Mons"). These volcanoes have been extinct for many millions of years,[[115]](#cite_note-ESAmarsvolcanoes-115) but the European *[Mars Express](/wiki/Mars_Express "Mars Express")* spacecraft has found evidence that volcanic activity may have occurred on Mars in the recent past as well.[[115]](#cite_note-ESAmarsvolcanoes-115)

[Jupiter](/wiki/Jupiter "Jupiter")'s [moon](/wiki/Natural_satellite "Natural satellite") [Io](/wiki/Io_(moon) "Io (moon)") is the most volcanically active object in the Solar System because of [tidal](/wiki/Tides "Tides") interaction with Jupiter. It is covered with volcanoes that erupt [sulfur](/wiki/Sulfur "Sulfur"), [sulfur dioxide](/wiki/Sulfur_dioxide "Sulfur dioxide") and [silicate](/wiki/Silicate "Silicate") rock, and as a result, [Io](/wiki/Io_(moon) "Io (moon)") is constantly being resurfaced. Its lavas are the hottest known anywhere in the Solar System, with temperatures exceeding 1,800 K (1,500 °C). In February 2001, the largest recorded volcanic eruptions in the Solar System occurred on Io.[[116]](#cite_note-116) [Europa](/wiki/Europa_(moon) "Europa (moon)"), the smallest of Jupiter's [Galilean moons](/wiki/Galilean_moon "Galilean moon"), also appears to have an active volcanic system, except that its volcanic activity is entirely in the form of water, which freezes into [ice](/wiki/Ice "Ice") on the frigid surface. This process is known as [cryovolcanism](/wiki/Cryovolcanism "Cryovolcanism"), and is apparently most common on the moons of the outer planets of the [Solar System](/wiki/Solar_System "Solar System").[[117]](#cite_note-117)

In 1989, the *[Voyager 2](/wiki/Voyager_2 "Voyager 2")* spacecraft observed [cryovolcanoes](/wiki/Cryovolcano "Cryovolcano") (ice volcanoes) on [Triton](/wiki/Triton_(moon) "Triton (moon)"), a [moon](/wiki/Natural_satellite "Natural satellite") of [Neptune](/wiki/Neptune "Neptune"), and in 2005 the *[Cassini–Huygens](/wiki/Cassini%E2%80%93Huygens "Cassini–Huygens")* probe photographed [fountains of frozen particles erupting from Enceladus](/wiki/Enceladus_(moon)#Cryovolcanism "Enceladus (moon)"), a moon of [Saturn](/wiki/Saturn "Saturn").[[118]](#cite_note-118)[[119]](#cite_note-119) The ejecta may be composed of water, [liquid nitrogen](/wiki/Liquid_nitrogen "Liquid nitrogen"), [ammonia](/wiki/Ammonia "Ammonia"), dust, or [methane](/wiki/Methane "Methane") compounds. *Cassini–Huygens* also found evidence of a methane-spewing cryovolcano on the [Saturnian](/wiki/Saturn "Saturn") moon [Titan](/wiki/Titan_(moon) "Titan (moon)"), which is believed to be a significant source of the methane found in its atmosphere.[[120]](#cite_note-120) It is theorized that cryovolcanism may also be present on the [Kuiper Belt Object](/wiki/Kuiper_Belt_Object "Kuiper Belt Object") [Quaoar](/wiki/Quaoar "Quaoar").

A 2010 study of the [exoplanet](/wiki/Exoplanet "Exoplanet") [COROT-7b](/wiki/COROT-7b "COROT-7b"), which was detected by [transit](/wiki/Transit_method "Transit method") in 2009, suggested that [tidal heating](/wiki/Tidal_heating "Tidal heating") from the host star very close to the planet and neighbouring planets could generate intense volcanic activity similar to that found on Io.[[121]](#cite_note-121)

## History of volcano understanding

Main article: [Volcanology § History](/wiki/Volcanology#History "Volcanology")

Volcanoes are not distributed evenly over the Earth's surface but active ones with significant impact were encountered early in human history, evidenced by footprints of [hominina](/wiki/Hominina "Hominina") found in East African volcanic ash dated at 3.66 million years old.[[122]](#cite_note-122): 104 The association of volcanoes with fire and disaster is found in many oral traditions and had religious and thus social significance before the first written record of concepts related to volcanoes. Examples are: (1) the stories in the Athabascan subcultures about humans living inside mountains and a woman who uses fire to escape from a mountain,[[123]](#cite_note-123): 135 (2) [Pele](/wiki/Pele_(deity) "Pele (deity)")'s migration through the Hawarian island chain, ability to destroy forests and manifestations of the god's temper,[[124]](#cite_note-124) and (3) the association in Javanese folklore of a king resident in [Mount Merapi](/wiki/Mount_Merapi "Mount Merapi") volcano and a queen resident at a beach 50 km (31 mi) away on what is now known to be an earthquake fault that interacts with that volcano.[[125]](#cite_note-125)

Many ancient accounts ascribe volcanic eruptions to [supernatural](/wiki/Supernatural "Supernatural") causes, such as the actions of [gods](/wiki/Gods "Gods") or [demigods](/wiki/Demigod "Demigod"). The earliest known such example is a neolithic goddess at [Çatalhöyük](/wiki/%C3%87atalh%C3%B6y%C3%BCk "Çatalhöyük").[[126]](#cite_note-Chester2007-126): 203 The Ancient Greek god [Hephaistos](/wiki/Hephaistos "Hephaistos") and the concepts of the underworld are aligned to volcanoes in that Greek culture.[[103]](#cite_note-Thomaidis2021-103)

However, others proposed more natural (but still incorrect) causes of volcanic activity. In the fifth century BC, [Anaxagoras](/wiki/Anaxagoras "Anaxagoras") proposed eruptions were caused by a great wind.[[127]](#cite_note-Sigurdsson2000-127) By 65 CE, [Seneca the Younger](/wiki/Seneca_the_Younger "Seneca the Younger") proposed combustion as the cause,[[127]](#cite_note-Sigurdsson2000-127) an idea also adopted by the [Jesuit](/wiki/Jesuit "Jesuit") [Athanasius Kircher](/wiki/Athanasius_Kircher "Athanasius Kircher") (1602–1680), who witnessed eruptions of [Mount Etna](/wiki/Mount_Etna "Mount Etna") and [Stromboli](/wiki/Stromboli "Stromboli"), then visited the crater of [Vesuvius](/wiki/Vesuvius "Vesuvius") and published his view of an Earth in *[Mundus Subterraneus](/wiki/Mundus_Subterraneus "Mundus Subterraneus")* with a central fire connected to numerous others depicting volcanoes as a type of safety valve.[[128]](#cite_note-128) Edward Jorden, in his work on mineral waters, challenged this view; in 1632 he proposed [sulfur](/wiki/Sulfur "Sulfur") "fermentation" as a heat source within Earth,[[127]](#cite_note-Sigurdsson2000-127) Astronomer [Johannes Kepler](/wiki/Johannes_Kepler "Johannes Kepler") (1571–1630) believed volcanoes were ducts for Earth's tears.[[129]](#cite_note-129)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] In 1650, [René Descartes](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9_Descartes "René Descartes") proposed the core of Earth was incandescent and, by 1785, the works of Decartes and others were synthesized into geology by [James Hutton](/wiki/James_Hutton "James Hutton") in his writings about [igneous intrusions](/wiki/Igneous_intrusion "Igneous intrusion") of magma.[[127]](#cite_note-Sigurdsson2000-127) [Lazzaro Spallanzani](/wiki/Lazzaro_Spallanzani "Lazzaro Spallanzani") had demonstrated by 1794 that steam explosions could cause explosive eruptions and many geologists held this as the universal cause of explosive eruptions up to the [1886 eruption of Mount Tarawera](/wiki/1886_eruption_of_Mount_Tarawera "1886 eruption of Mount Tarawera") which allowed in one event differentiation of the concurrent [phreatomagmatic](/wiki/Phreatomagmatic "Phreatomagmatic") and [hydrothermal](/wiki/Hydrothermal "Hydrothermal") eruptions from dry explosive eruption, of, as it turned out, a basalt [dyke](/wiki/Dike_(geology) "Dike (geology)").[[130]](#cite_note-130): 16–18[[131]](#cite_note-131): 4 [Alfred Lacroix](/wiki/Alfred_Lacroix "Alfred Lacroix") built upon his other knowledge with his studies on the [1902 eruption of Mount Pelée](/wiki/1902_eruption_of_Mount_Pel%C3%A9e "1902 eruption of Mount Pelée"),[[127]](#cite_note-Sigurdsson2000-127) and by 1928 [Arthur Holmes](/wiki/Arthur_Holmes "Arthur Holmes") work had brought together the concepts of radioactive generation of heat, Earth's [mantle](/wiki/Mantle_(geology) "Mantle (geology)") structure, partial decompression melting of magma, and magma convection.[[127]](#cite_note-Sigurdsson2000-127) This eventually led to the acceptance of plate tectonics.[[132]](#cite_note-132)
